'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わるに民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]。
[[1981年]][[1月21日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[秋保の田植踊]]
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:岩手県|やまやのたうえおとり]] [[category:日本の文化|やまやのたうえおとり]] [[Category:伝統芸能|やまやのたうえおとり]]

[[1981年]][[1月21日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わるに民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]。
[[1981年]][[1月21日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[秋保の田植踊]]
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:岩手県|やまやのたうえおとり]] [[Category:伝統芸能|やまやのたうえおとり]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財|やまやのたうえおとり]]

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わるに民俗芸能、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
田植踊りは、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「予祝」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、「山屋の田植踊」は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊りである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]

revision.text:
'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わるに民俗芸能、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
田植踊りは、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、「山屋の田植踊」は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊りである。

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わるに民俗芸能、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
田植踊りは、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、「山屋の田植踊」は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊りである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまやのおうえおとり}} [[Category:岩手県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:紫波町]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、平成5年。2ｐ</ref>。
始まりは明確ではないが、[[奥州藤原氏]]の頃に[[砂金]]採取のため来住した孫六なる者が踊る田楽、田舞を土地の豪族菅原氏が里人に伝承を受けさせたことにはじまるという<ref name=":0" />。また、由来書によると孫六は[[京都府|京]]の[[伏見区|伏見]]の人で山屋乙女石峠の八枚金山の支配人として、芸事に優れた人であったという<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=平成4年 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>。
毎年小正月の15日に庭元の田の神様に一行が集まり、笠揃えの行事を行い一年の田植踊が始まる習慣となっており、2月までの冬の期間旦那衆の家々を豊作祈願をしながら連日のように廻ったといわれる<ref name=":1" />。田植踊りは、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、「山屋の田植踊」は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊りである。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references>

== 外部リンク == * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}}

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、平成5年。2ｐ</ref>。
始まりは明確ではないが、[[奥州藤原氏]]の頃に[[砂金]]採取のため来住した孫六なる者が踊る田楽、田舞を土地の豪族菅原氏が里人に伝承を受けさせたことにはじまるという<ref name=":0" />。また、由来書によると孫六は[[京都府|京]]の[[伏見区|伏見]]在の山屋乙女石峠八枚金山の支配人で、芸事に優れた人であったという<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=平成4年 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>。
毎年小正月の15日に庭元の田の神様に一行が集まり、笠揃えの行事を行い一年の田植踊が始まる習慣となっており、2月までの冬の期間旦那衆の家々を豊作祈願をしながら連日のように廻ったといわれる<ref name=":1" />。田植踊は、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、本田植踊は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊である。

毎年小正月の15日に庭元の田の神様に一行が集まり、笠揃えの行事を行い一年の田植踊が始まる習慣となっており、2月までの冬の期間旦那衆の家々を豊作祈願をしながら連日のように廻ったといわれる<ref name=":1" />。田植踊は、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、本田植踊は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊である。

{{DEFAULTSORT:やまやのおうえおとり}} [[Category:岩手県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:紫波町]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]山屋に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、平成5年。</ref>。
== 概要 == 始まりは明確ではないが、[[奥州藤原氏]]の頃に[[砂金]]採取のため来住した孫六なる者が踊る田楽、田舞を土地の豪族菅原氏が里人に伝承を受けさせたことにはじまるという<ref name=":0" />。また、由来書によると孫六は[[京都府|京]]の[[伏見区|伏見]]在の山屋乙女石峠八枚金山の支配人で、芸事に優れた人であったという<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=平成4年 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>。付近には「遠山田植踊」や「舟久保さんさ踊」といった同じく金採掘に関わる由緒を持つ芸能がある<ref name=":0" />。
毎年小正月の15日に庭元の田の神様に一行が集まり、笠揃えの行事を行い一年の田植踊が始まる習慣となっており、2月までの冬の期間旦那衆の家々を豊作祈願をしながら連日のように廻ったといわれる<ref name=":1" />。田植踊は、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、本田植踊は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊である。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references>
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[奥州藤原氏]] *[[紫波町]]
== 外部リンク == * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまやのおうえおとり}} [[Category:岩手県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:紫波町]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]の山屋地区に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、平成5年。</ref>。

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|en|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>. == Overview == The or..."
revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|en|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|en|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|en|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|en|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|en|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references>

== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]
[[Category:Iwate Prefecture festivals]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Yoshuku-geino]] [[Category:Shiwa, Iwate]] {{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:Ōshū Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[ja:山屋の田植踊]]

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|en|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|en|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|en|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|en|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|en|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.

The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references>

== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references>
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}} * {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.

The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|Koshogatsu|ja|小正月}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references> == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara clan|ja|奥州藤原氏}} * {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|ja|紫波町}}
== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]

19 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:山屋の田植踊 up to rev 96594964
revision.text:
'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]の山屋地区に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>。
== 概要 == 始まりの時期は明確ではないが、[[奥州藤原氏]]の頃に[[砂金]]採取のため来住した孫六なる者が踊る田楽、田舞を土地の豪族菅原氏が里人に伝承を受けさせたことにはじまると伝わる<ref name=":0" />。また、由来書によると孫六は[[京都府|京]]の[[伏見区|伏見]]在の山屋乙女石峠八枚金山の支配人で、芸事に優れた人であったという<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>。山屋地区を流れる[[天王川]]の源流域は[[女牛鉱山]]竪金鉱床に近接し、中流域には長福金山もあり、当時これらの鉱床に由来する砂金が採掘されていた可能性があり<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>、付近には「遠山田植踊」や「舟久保さんさ踊」といった同じく金採掘に関わる由緒を持つ芸能がある<ref name=":0" />。
毎年小正月の15日に庭元の田の神様に一行が集まり、笠揃えの行事を行い一年の田植踊が始まる習慣となっており、2月までの冬の期間旦那衆の家々を豊作祈願をしながら連日のように廻ったといわれる<ref name=":1" />。田植踊は、稲田の作業を舞踊化し真似ることで、その年の豊年を祈願する「[[予祝芸能|予祝]]」の意味を持つ芸能で、一般的には正月期間に行われ、屋外で演じる「庭田植え」と、室内で演じる「座敷田植え」があるが、本田植踊は早乙女の笠ふりと間をつなぐ仲踊が中心となり、座敷で踊る「座敷田植え」と呼ばれるものである。
前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊である。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references>
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[奥州藤原氏]] *[[紫波町]]
== 外部リンク == * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 奈良文化財研究所全国遺跡報告総覧「町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書」2023、紫波町教育委員会]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまやのおうえおとり}} [[Category:岩手県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:紫波町]] {{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:奥州藤原氏]] [[Category:紫波町の歴史]]

revision.text:
'''山屋の田植踊'''（やまやのたうえおどり）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]の山屋地区に伝わる民俗芸能。[[昭和]]56年（[[1981年]]）に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993年。</ref>。

前口上から始まり「三番叟」から、1年の稲作過程を歌と踊りで順々に演じてみせる。さらに、囃子舞などの余興も加えられ進行に興が添えられる。初春の予祝の行事が風流化され、きわめて多彩な芸内容を持った田植踊である。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references>
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[奥州藤原氏]] *[[紫波町]]
== 外部リンク == * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 奈良文化財研究所全国遺跡報告総覧「町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書」2023、紫波町教育委員会]

revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|qq=}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|qq=}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|qq=}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references> == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=日本の伝統芸能}} * [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]] * [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]]
== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]
{{japan-culture-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|山屋の田植踊|version=96594964|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|qq=}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.

Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
