According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11467783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|qq=|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references> == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}} * [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]] * [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]]
== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]
{{japan-culture-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|山屋の田植踊|version=96594964|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." [[ja:山屋の田植踊]] [[Category:Pre-celebration Performing Arts]] [[Category:Ōshu-Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Festivals in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Shīwa]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11467783}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11467783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references> == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}} * [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]] * [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]]
== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]

Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. [[ja:山屋の田植踊]] [[Category:Pre-celebration Performing Arts]] [[Category:Ōshu-Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Festivals in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Shīwa]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11467783}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11467783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
== References == <references group=""></references> == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}} * [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]] * [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]]
== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]

The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11467783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of [[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa, Iwate]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]

It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara clan]]. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of [[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshogatsu]], performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

== External Links == * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|10|山屋の田植踊楽}} * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/131866 Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties: "Town Gold Mine Ruins Distribution Survey Report," 2023, Shiwa Town Board of Education]

The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山屋の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11467783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|First Full Moon Festival|en|First Full Moon Festival|WD=Q13423452|lt=Koshogatsu}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|First Full Moon Festival|en|First Full Moon Festival|WD=Q13423452|lt=Koshogatsu}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.

revision.text:
'''Yamaya no Taue Odori''' (山屋の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a type of folk performance passed down in the Yamaya district of {{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa, Iwate|1=Shiwa, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}. It was designated as a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} in 1981 (Shōwa 56)<ref name=":0">『わたしたちの文化財』紫波町教育委員会、1993rd year 。</ref>.
== Overview ==
The origins of this dance are unclear, but it is said to have begun during the time of the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|1=Northern Fujiwara}}. According to legend, a man named Magoroku, who came to the region for {{ill|gold panning|ja|砂金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gold panning|1=placer gold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q68139832}}, performed rice-planting dances and passed them on to the locals under the guidance of the Sugawara clan, a powerful family in the area<ref name=":0" />. According to other records, Magoroku hailed from Kyoto's Fushimi and managed the Yamaya Otomeishi Pass Gold Mine, known for his artistic skills<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書1992 紫波町の文化財 |year=1992 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=77}}</ref>.
The area surrounding the source of the Tennogawa River in Yamaya is close to the Jogyu Mine and Chofuku Gold Mine. It is believed that gold sand was extracted from these deposits, as supported by cultural asset surveys<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=紫波町文化財調査報告書2022 町内金鉱山遺跡詳細分布調査報告書 |year=2023 |publisher=紫波町教育委員会 |pages= |author= |page=5}}</ref>. Related arts in the vicinity, such as the "Toyama Taue Odori" and "Funakubo Sansa Odori," share origins connected to gold mining<ref name=":0" />.
Every year, on the 15th day of {{ill|First Full Moon Festival|en|First Full Moon Festival|WD=Q13423452|lt=Koshogatsu|1=First Full Moon Festival}}, performers gather at the Tanokamisama shrine of the host household to perform the "Kasa Soroe" ritual, marking the beginning of the year's rice-planting dances. Traditionally, during the winter months until February, performers visited the homes of patrons daily, offering prayers for abundant harvests<ref name=":1" />.
The Taue Odori depicts the agricultural process of rice planting, turning it into a ritualistic performance to invoke bountiful harvests, characteristic of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It features two main styles: outdoor performances known as "Niwa Taue" and indoor performances known as "Zashiki Taue." The Yamaya no Taue Odori is primarily a "Zashiki Taue," focusing on dances led by the Saotome performers, with intermediary dances tying the performances together.
The performance begins with an introductory speech, followed by "Sanbasō," and sequentially portrays the year's rice-planting activities through songs and dances. The event is further enlivened with festive entertainment such as Hayashi dances. As a New Year's celebratory ritual, the Yamaya no Taue Odori is a sophisticated and diverse expression of rice-planting culture.
